annotations.comment: I’ll be honest in saying this title feels a little “erotica” ish. Sorry!

paragraphs.body: The sea beckoned me since girlhood, the way war calls out to a soldier. I still remember the wind's soft, airy voice whispering my name as a child; the tide begging me to come to her, to the shore, where water and land converse, and let the waves lap over my small body—let the waves drag me out to sea. Father loved the sea as I did. Many mornings I awoke to strong, calloused hands gripping my shoulder and tobacco-stained teeth grinning through the darkness. Mother sighed disappointedly upon our return, another set of pajamas soaked by salt water and dripping in the scent of fish. Father had always been a sailor; salt water ran through his veins and down into the truest parts of him. So, when the men from his crew came knocking, caps in hand, I did not cry.

annotations.text: war calls out to a soldier.
annotations.comment: This is a load metaphor, which I think works well for you.
Maybe this is “the sea breeze” or something that locates it in the water?
Nice image. I confess that I’m always suspicious of characters going by “Father” or “Mother” — feels a little fake to me.
Unclear what he’s doing.
You don’t need the adverb here.

annotations.text: down into the truest parts of him

annotations.comment: I like the slightly obscure way you phrase this. I was a little confused in learning that he had a ship and a crew — perhaps that could be mentioned earlier?

paragraphs.body: Mother and I both knew the waves stole her daughter that day it took her husband. I was never an exceptionally intelligent child. Unfocused, unmotivated, unable to excel in anything other than roughhousing. Rather unlady-like too, both in my appearance and attitude. Girls found me tomboyish, boys found me ugly. I wanted to be like those girls the boys sought after: so effortlessly feminine, so easily loved. But no. I could not be the daughter Mother longed to raise. I could not be married off. Neither delicate nor graceful: unworthy. Following in Father’s footsteps proved to be fate. I found myself at the age of eighteen standing on a wood-rotten dock Father had taken me to many times before, patiently awaiting the arrival of a ship. For hours I sat on that dock, curled up, head resting on my arms. Just waiting. I remember waking late that night to sudden wetness engulfing my body, and once I squeezed the salt from my eyes, a ship rocked on waves. Gruff, weathered men gallivanted over the dock, the stench of tuna emanating out of their pores as they marched to the nearest bar.

annotations.text: stole her daughter that day it took her husband
annotations.comment: Unclear what this means since the narrator already loved the sea.
A small note, but you dedicate about five sentences to getting this across, and I think just one or two is enough. (I also didn’t quite believe that the narrator wanted to be like those girls!)
I’m a little confused here — is she waiting for a ship or for a specific ship?

paragraphs.body: Their captain looked like my father.

I wipe the sweat from my brow as I drop the last crate of bait onto the damp deck. A few grizzled men lug heavier crates behind me, crates they shooed me from, and as the sun falls behind the horizon and paints the sky pink and orange, I show myself to my sleeping quarters. My body sways and jerks as the engine sputters into a roar and begins to pull the ship from the dock. Aches racks my being, and exhaustion melds with gravity, pulling me to the cot laid out on its own. This voyage marks my third year with the crew. We catch fish, usually along the Atlantic coast of the colonies, but tonight we start for the Mediterranean. Somewhere near Italy. Better fish, Captain says. It does not matter to me, I just fish where I am told. Whether sweat beads escape from every orifice or Poseidon sends a typhoon, I fish.

annotations.comment: Nice small acknowledgement of her status as a woman.
I’d like to have some sense of the time period of this story. It’s hard to place, since fishing and seafaring is such an ancient thing.
annotations.context_left: I wipe the sweat from my brow as I drop the last crate of bait onto the damp de
annotations.context_right: . A few grizzled men lug heavier crates behind me, crates they shooed me from, and as the sun falls

annotations.comment: I wondered if this is possible? Can an American ship just take off and fish in another country’s waters? I also want to note some small confusion here — you have her obsessed with the ocean in the first page, but here, she seems a little more obsessed with fishing. Is the latter the means to being near the former?

paragraphs.body: “I don’t get paid enough for this. Especially when the girl makes us pick up her slack,” one man grumbles outside my door. The men always complain. They complain about their joints, about their children waiting patiently for their return, about wives they long to touch, about booze and the lack thereof. They complain about me, about my body and its poorly executed femininity—You are nothing to look at. You ought to make an effort to be pretty since you are the only woman here. Why do you have such muscles? How can you even call yourself a woman? You are scarred and calloused and no good. Your hair is frizzy and unkept. You have a big, ugly nose. Why are you not pretty?—But I fish well anyhow.

annotations.comment: This made me smile. Now I will note that I’m about 1 1/2 pages in on a 5 page story, and it’s beautifully written. But I’m also a little unclear on what the plot or conflict are here. The narrator seems to be fairly content in her life (which is nice!) — despite the grumbling, she has what she has always wanted. So, what’s the story?

paragraphs.body: Something large and weighty plummets into the water outside my grimy window.

I sit upright on my lonely cot. Rising, I move to the door and pull it open just enough to make out a sliver of the deck. The men have gone to sleep. It is quiet. Barefoot, I trudge out towards the starboard edge of the ship and lean my body over the side. There is a body in the water, sinking—no, it is being pulled—toward the sea bed. A little further down, there is a soft white glow; a feminine aura exudes from the light, and I know now just how much I lacked it. The glow fades, and I remain peering into the sea.

annotations.comment: Again, beautifully written. I would like a bit more here, though — I hadn’t really sensed a lack before this moment, so I don’t quite see how this sighting (I assume of a mermaid) is meaningful to the narrator.
annotations.context_left: lean my body over the side. There is a body in the water, sinking—no, it is being pulled—toward the
annotations.context_right: bed. A little further down, there is a soft white glow; a feminine aura exudes from the light, and

paragraphs.body: Captain swears as I inform him of last night’s casualty. Sirens are quite common in these waters, but he did not expect to lose men so quickly. Apparently, the man that fell (jumped) was one of five victims tricked by her sultry song. All we can do is pray, Captain says, but to whom do I pray? Poseidon? That is who the men pray to; but if the siren is under his rule, won’t my prayers go unanswered? Perhaps the siren herself will listen. So I fish as the sun rises and pray as she sets. I can hear the men pray each night.

annotations.comment: The implication here is that she waited several hours, which seems unlikely. Might we know which person was lost? I wondered if it was the guy who was such an ass to her?
Yikes, how big is this ship? That feels like a lot of men!

I’m not clear on who is saying the  prayer that follows. How does the narrator feel about the Siren? Is she scared or is the Siren only a threat to men? As I read on, I get it, the prayer is somehow collectively said by the men. I would encourage you to consider diversifying their response a bit more — maybe not all of them hate her, etc. Because one big question here is why she is allowed to pursue this work — if the captain is not her father, why did he let her come on board at all?
annotations.context_left: n. So I fish as the

paragraphs.body: “Poseidon, god of the sea, hear us now. If your sirens must feed, let us feed them. We will give you our weak. We shall cast her overboard if it pleases you. Oh, great god of the sea, hear our prayers. Have mercy on our souls. Take the girl instead.” The men sleep peacefully once they’ve claimed me as a sacrifice. I have learned to sleep quickly, and to barricade the door if I sleep. It is anything but peaceful. When I hear their prayers, I kneel and begin my own. I pray that the glowing girl will take mercy on me if the men throw me overboard. I pray that she will make my death quick if they do make me their sacrifice. I pray that she may take them instead of me, but I will not hold out hope.

annotations.comment: The situation here seems a lot more dire than what we were shown. I’m sure that’s partly because of the Siren, but again, I’m hung up on how she got on this ship at all, if everyone hates her?

paragraphs.body: Night after night, the siren lurks outside the men’s sleeping quarters. Crying, wailing, begging them to come into the water. The death toll rises and rises. Captain does not drop anchor anymore. We sail through the night, but it does not stop them from jumping. Talk of wives and children do nothing to stop the jumping. The other men scream and shake them, trying desperately to snap them out of their trance, but nothing ever stops them from jumping. Once or twice, I watched her glow pause before my window, but after a moment, she dove beneath the waves.

annotations.text: The death toll rises and rises.
annotations.comment: I wonder why they don’t try to ancient technique of stopping their ears? That’s what Odysseus did.
Is she trying to keep them from jumping too? An aspect of the story that’s confusing is that the narrator is mostly just watching the plot. She doesn’t really seem to be actively involved.

paragraphs.body: “There’s someone else you can take instead! On the other side of the ship! The worst of us all! Then will you be satisfied?” The mens’ voices jumble together as they plead. I will become their sacrifice after all. Perhaps I should have prayed to Poseidon; perhaps she was not his siren. I clothe myself and follow her glow out to the deck. She is, admittedly, more beautiful than I assumed: eyes so blue they appear gray under the pale moonlight; smooth skin caresses the divots of her ribs, soft shadows catching beneath highlighted peaks of bone; white hair floats around her with the ebb and flow of waves, framing her face perfectly with each movement. An understanding washes over me, and, before she can even call out, I want to jump. There is something so welcoming about her feminine presence, something I had long forgotten after all these years surrounded by men harboring such hate for my being. I want to tell her of her beauty, to inform her of her effortlessness, to make certain she knows—but that would be foolish, of course she knows; the men clamber off the side of the ship to be consumed by it, they toss their wives and children aside for a chance at the life she promises; my words would lend her no solace.

annotations.comment: Can you clarify why this moment is the one that convinces her she must do something? It feels like the men have been yelling such things for a while. Why does she at this point decide to go out?

annotations.text: follow her glow out to the deck.
annotations.comment: Wording is confusing — how does she follow her glow?
So even though she’s not very good and not really liked, she’s been doing this for years?

paragraphs.body: Thick quiet hangs between us, and I press my body against the banister. Her gray-blue eyes contract into slits. She stares, parts soft pink lips, pauses, then continues—You are beautiful. She sounds like the waves, like the tide, the shore, the salt in the sea, like an anchor dragging along the ocean’s floor, like grains of sand beneath feet, like tackle and fishing lines, the give of decaying docks, like seagulls, like sprats shooting up in a frenzied flail, the scream as one brushes against a hand, the laughter right after, the hand plunging back into water, the splash of bodies throwing themselves to the sea, the sigh of a mother who just spent hours painstakingly scrubbing the fish smell out of everything else, and the kissing of cheeks and foreheads after an affectionate scolding; she sounds like the love effortless girls receive, a love I was never worthy of—a love I do deserve, a love I don’t need, a beauty I do possess, a femininity I do embody, everything I have never been, everything I will never be, and all that I am. I push myself from the edge. These sirens do not serve Poseidon. They do not need guidance. They require no solace in the form of men, for they are their own solace—as I am my own. “You are beautiful too,” I tell her. The siren’s pink lips smile as she dives beneath the waves.

annotations.comment: This is an interesting but also confusing moment. It’s as if you’ve written an epiphany as the moment the conflict resolves, which is an interesting technique. But the language is perhaps a bit too poetic — the referral back to the mother, for example, is a lot to shove into this moment — and I wasn’t sure about the struggle that was resolved. I do like the final moment, but I also was like, wait, if the narrator had talked to the Siren earlier, could she have saved the men? Should she have? Zoe, I enjoyed this first draft. You obviously are a writer with gifts, and while stories about merpeople and sirens have become strangely commonplace lately, I enjoyed this one. Your evocation of the sea is engaging, and who wouldn’t be rooting for your unnamed narrator? As I noted you have a story that follows a lot of fiction’s beats but doesn’t quite include a conflict that’s clearly shown. Your narrator is oppressed by the men she works with, and experiences a moment of solidarity with a magical creature, which seems to loosen something in her. However, what we don’t get to see are the details about the situation she’s in — we’re told that she waits for a boards a ship, and then suddenly it’s years later. I couldn’t figure out how and why this employment of hers was allowed to continue, since she didn’t seem to be very good at the job and she definitely wasn’t liked. You need to think about the specifics that would make that possible. I’d also encourage you to ponder how the story might work better if she is active in trying to fight (or help!) the Siren, instead of sort of hanging out until the time you’ve decided is right for her to step up. What is the battle going on here? How can she be a participant in that battle? Again, this is strong work, and frankly, plot is the hardest part of writing for most newer fiction writers. So don’t be discouraged — just think about what makes sense for what you want the story to be!
